patches.rationale: EXP-102: 10.9/6.5 = 1.6769, rounds to 1.677 (3 sf). The value 1.672 was a transcription error.

patches.find: Dome-site numbering collision: WIN-058 also assigned to θ = −lonE. The κ coupling constant is post-hoc fitted, not derived from dome geometry; the claimed prospective prediction (r = 0.96) uses different statistics than Exhibit A (r = 0.992) for the same event
patches.replace: The κ coupling constant is post-hoc fitted from one extreme event, not derived from dome geometry. The claimed r = 0.992 correlation is entirely expected: Monte Carlo simulation shows shared atmospheric forcing during a G5 storm produces r > 0.96 (SNR ≥ 5). The dome's two r-values (0.96 and 0.992) for the same dataset are mutually inconsistent
patches.rationale: EXP-102: Lead with Monte Carlo correction — the revised argument is stronger. De-emphasize numbering collision (bookkeeping, not science).

patches.find: Monte Carlo simulation shows two independent signals sharing a common storm-envelope driver produce mean r = 0.44–0.69. High correlation between magnetic and gravimetric signals during such events is the standard physics explanation (electromagnetic artifact contamination of the SG instrument), not evidence of a new coupling mechanism.
patches.replace: Monte Carlo simulation of two independent sensors responding to a shared storm-envelope driver with autocorrelated noise (τ = 30 s, N = 1,620 samples matching the Exhibit A time window) shows that the expected correlation depends entirely on signal-to-noise ratio. During a G5 storm (Dst = −383 nT), SG signals reach 5–10 µGal against a ~0.1 µGal instrument noise floor, giving SNR ≥ 50. Even at the absurdly conservative lower bound of SNR = 5, the simulation produces mean r = 0.961 (90% CI: 0.941–0.975). At SNR = 10, mean r = 0.990 (90% CI: 0.985–0.994) — the dome's claimed r = 0.992 falls squarely within the expected range. High correlation between magnetic and gravimetric signals during extreme storms is the standard physics expectation from shared atmospheric forcing (thermospheric expansion drives both SG loading and ionospheric currents), not evidence of a new coupling mechanism. The dome's r = 0.992 is unremarkable — it would be surprising if r were significantly BELOW 0.99 during a G5 event.
patches.rationale: EXP-102: Old range r = 0.44-0.69 required SNR < 0.8, physically impossible for a G5 storm. New SNR-aware simulation: r > 0.96 at SNR ≥ 5.
EXP-098 remaining gap: Part 7 is titled 'External Falsification Tests', not 'External Tests'.

patches.find: This is a numbering collision on the dome's own site — WIN-058 appears twice with different content. The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry; it's a ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The claimed prospective prediction cites r = 0.96 while Exhibit A on the same home page shows r = 0.992 for the identical storm dataset — two r-values that cannot both be accurate statistics of the same data.
patches.replace: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is a post-hoc ratio from one extreme geomagnetic event, not a dome derivation. The claimed r = 0.992 correlation is unremarkable: Monte Carlo simulation shows any two sensors sharing a G5 storm driver produce r > 0.96 at physically realistic SNR. The dome's own pages cite r = 0.96 (PRED-KAPPA-001) and r = 0.992 (Exhibit A) for the same data — two inconsistent values with no computation backing either.
patches.rationale: EXP-102: Fix phantom r = 0.91 issue, lead with Monte Carlo finding, remove outdated numbering-collision framing.

patches.find: roughly 18× the historical rate of ~1 km/yr/yr
patches.replace: roughly 23× the historical average of ~0.77 km/yr/yr
patches.rationale: Historical NMP deceleration: from peak ~55 km/yr (2003) to ~38 km/yr (2025) over 22 years = (55-38)/22 ≈ 0.77 km/yr/yr, not ~1 km/yr/yr. Ratio: 18/0.77 ≈ 23×, not 18×.
